title: Jamaican beef patties
subtitle: Spiced beef mince in flaky turmeric pastry
excerpt: Flaky, turmeric-yellow shortcrust pastry wrapped around a filling of curried beef mince with scotch bonnet and thyme. The definitive Jamaican street food, sold from every bakery and market stall.
sourceNotes: Jamaican beef patties trace their lineage to Cornish pasties brought by British settlers, transformed by Jamaican spicing (curry, scotch bonnet, allspice, and thyme) and given their distinctive yellow pastry by turmeric. They are sold from every Jamaican bakery and lunch counter. The filling uses curry powder in the Jamaican style: a robust blend used more generously than in Indian cookery. The pastry must be short, flaky, and barely sweet.

content.text: A good Jamaican beef patty has three things right. The pastry is short and flaky with a clear turmeric yellow. The filling is deeply spiced and slightly moist but not wet. The two come together in a thin, neatly crimped crescent that holds its shape when you bite. The patty is not a pasty and not an empanada. The pastry is thinner, the filling more assertively seasoned, and the whole thing smaller than it looks.

Cook the filling first and let it cool completely before assembling. Hot filling steams the pastry from the inside and you lose the flakiness that makes the thing worth eating. The dough comes together quickly and rests for 30 minutes in the fridge, which relaxes the gluten and makes it easier to roll without shrinking.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a frying pan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 5 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet, {{thyme-fresh}} of fresh thyme, {{curry-powder}} of curry powder, and {{allspice}} of allspice. Stir and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{beef-mince}} of beef mince and break it up with a spoon. Cook for 8 minutes until browned through and the liquid has evaporated. Stir in the {{breadcrumbs-dried}} of dried breadcrumbs to bind the filling. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Spread onto a plate and leave to cool completely.

Combine the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour, {{turmeric}} of turmeric, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt in a large bowl. Rub in the {{unsalted-butter}} of cold butter until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs with some larger flakes of butter remaining, those flakes are what gives the pastry its flakiness, so don't overwork it. Add 80 to 100 ml of cold water, a little at a time, mixing with a knife until the dough just comes together. Press into a flat disc, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. On a lightly floured surface, roll the pastry to 3 mm thick. Cut out circles about 15 cm in diameter, a small plate works as a guide. Place 2 to 3 tablespoons of the cooled filling on one half of each circle, leaving a 1 cm border. Brush the border with beaten egg, fold the pastry over, and press the edges together firmly. Crimp with a fork or pinch to seal. Place on a baking tray lined with baking paper. Brush the tops with the remaining egg wash and bake for 30 to 35 minutes until deep golden.

items.fix: Let the dough rest at room temperature for 5 minutes before rolling. If it cracks when you try to fold it, press the crack together and continue, the egg wash will seal the edge.
items.cause: Dough too cold or too dry

items.fix: The filling must be completely cool and fairly dry before filling. Press the edges firmly and crimp with a fork rather than just pinching.
items.cause: Filling too wet; edges not properly sealed

items.fix: Leave pea-sized chunks of butter in the mix. Stop mixing as soon as the dough holds together. Rest in the fridge before rolling, cold pastry holds its structure in the oven.
items.cause: Butter worked in too thoroughly; pastry overworked

items.fix: Taste the filling before assembling. Jamaican patties are assertively spiced. Increase curry powder to 3 tsp and add a pinch more allspice.
items.cause: Not enough curry powder or allspice; scotch bonnet removed

content.text: Jamaican beef patties are lunch-counter food, sold wrapped in paper from glass cases kept warm all day. In Jamaica they are eaten at any hour: breakfast with a coco bread (the patty slid inside like a sandwich), lunch standing at the counter, a quick dinner before the evening shift. In the UK they are sold from Jamaican bakeries and Caribbean food shops. The patty is one of the more successful Jamaican exports precisely because it travels well: it holds its shape, reheats without damage, and makes sense to anyone who has ever eaten a pastry.
